steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone with biliary obstruction causing cholestatic hepatitis; biliary colic without cholecystitis
ensemble.information_gap: Whether there is biliary ductal dilation or a common bile duct stone (choledocholithiasis) to explain the markedly elevated transaminases, elevated direct bilirubin, and elevated alkaline phosphatase, given that the outside ultrasound showed gallstones but no gallbladder wall thickening and did not formally characterize the biliary ductal anatomy at this institution
ensemble.expected_finding: Common bile duct dilation (>6-7 mm) with or without a visible choledocholith, or alternatively, gallbladder wall thickening/pericholecystic fluid suggesting cholecystitis; the physician is most hoping to see ductal dilation pointing toward choledocholithiasis
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The combination of RUQ pain, known gallstones, markedly elevated ALT/AST (>500), elevated direct bilirubin (1.6 mg/dL), and elevated alkaline phosphatase (220) strongly raises concern for biliary obstruction from a CBD stone, and a dedicated institutional abdominal ultrasound is the appropriate first-line imaging to assess biliary ductal anatomy and gallbladder status before proceeding to ERCP consultation.
grounding: History: 'transferred to ___ for consideration of ERCP consultation given LFT abnormalities'
History: 'Ultrasound showed cholelithiasis without gallbladder wall thickening'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a classic biochemical pattern of biliary obstruction — dramatically elevated transaminases (ALT 528, AST 275), elevated direct bilirubin (1.6), and elevated alkaline phosphatase (220) — in the setting of known gallstones and RUQ pain, making choledocholithiasis the leading concern. The outside ultrasound confirmed stones but did not report on the CBD caliber, and a dedicated institutional ultrasound is needed to assess for ductal dilation or a visible CBD stone before ERCP can be planned. Normal lipase (23) makes pancreatitis unlikely, and absence of GB wall thickening on outside imaging makes uncomplicated cholecystitis less likely, directing attention to the biliary duct itself.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone with biliary obstruction; biliary colic with secondary hepatocellular injury
ensemble.information_gap: The outside ultrasound showed cholelithiasis but no gallbladder wall thickening, and no mention was made of the common bile duct caliber or presence of a CBD stone — yet the markedly elevated transaminases (ALT 528, AST 275), elevated alkaline phosphatase (220), and elevated direct bilirubin (1.6) strongly suggest biliary obstruction rather than simple cholecystitis or colic alone; the physician needs formal hepatobiliary ultrasound to evaluate the biliary tree, specifically CBD diameter and any visible stone.
ensemble.expected_finding: Dilated common bile duct (>6–7 mm) and/or a visible choledocholithiasis, with or without intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation, to confirm biliary obstruction as the cause of the LFT pattern; alternatively, findings of acute cholecystitis (wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign) not captured on the outside study.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The outside ultrasound was limited (reported only cholelithiasis without wall thickening, with no CBD assessment documented), yet the patient's LFT profile — markedly elevated ALT/AST, elevated alkaline phosphatase, elevated direct bilirubin, and urobilinogen 2.0 — demands formal evaluation of the biliary tree at this institution before ERCP planning.
grounding: History: 'Ultrasound showed cholelithiasis without gallbladder wall thickening' — outside study did not characterize CBD
History: 'transferred to ___ for consideration of ERCP consultation given LFT abnormalities'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has known gallstones, RUQ pain, and a cholestatic-hepatocellular LFT pattern (markedly elevated ALT/AST with elevated ALP, direct bilirubin, and urobilinogen) strongly suggesting a stone has migrated into the common bile duct causing biliary obstruction — the primary reason she was transferred for ERCP consideration. The outside ultrasound only commented on gallbladder stones without wall thickening and did not characterize the CBD, so a formal hepatobiliary ultrasound at this institution is the rational first step to assess CBD diameter, intrahepatic biliary dilation, and any visible choledocholithiasis, as well as to reassess the gallbladder for evolving cholecystitis, before committing to ERCP.

grounding: History: 'Ultrasound showed cholelithiasis without gallbladder wall thickening'

The outside ultrasound documented gallstones but did not characterize the common bile duct, so a dedicated repeat ultrasound at this institution is the logical first step to assess for CBD dilation or a visible ductal stone before committing to ERCP, while also re-evaluating the gallbladder itself for signs of acute cholecystitis that may have been missed.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stone) with biliary obstruction causing transaminitis and hyperbilirubinemia; biliary colic without cholecystitis
ensemble.information_gap: Whether there is choledocholithiasis (common bile duct dilation or visible stone) or cholecystitis (wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign) to explain the markedly elevated LFTs and direct hyperbilirubinemia — the outside ultrasound only documented cholelithiasis without gallbladder wall thickening and was performed at an outside institution without images available for review
ensemble.expected_finding: Common bile duct dilation (>6–7 mm) suggesting choledocholithiasis, or gallbladder wall thickening/pericholecystic fluid suggesting cholecystitis; alternatively, confirmation of a non-dilated CBD which would shift management away from urgent ERCP
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The outside ultrasound showing cholelithiasis without wall thickening was performed at an external facility and its images are unavailable; given markedly elevated transaminases (ALT 528, AST 275), direct hyperbilirubinemia (direct bili 1.6), elevated alkaline phosphatase (220), and the reason for transfer being ERCP consultation, a dedicated hepatobiliary ultrasound at this institution is necessary to evaluate for CBD dilation or other biliary pathology before proceeding with invasive intervention.
grounding: History: 'transferred to ___ for consideration of ERCP consultation given LFT abnormalities'

ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a cholestatic-pattern LFT elevation (markedly elevated ALT/AST with elevated ALP and direct hyperbilirubinemia) in the context of known gallstones and RUQ pain, raising strong concern for choledocholithiasis as the driver of her transaminitis and the indication for ERCP. The outside ultrasound only documented gallstones without cholecystitis findings, but did not report on common bile duct diameter — the critical piece of information needed before ERCP planning. A dedicated in-house hepatobiliary ultrasound is the logical next step to assess CBD caliber, look for intrahepatic ductal dilation, and re-evaluate the gallbladder, thereby either confirming choledocholithiasis to proceed with ERCP or identifying an alternative explanation.

verification.actual_finding: The common hepatic duct measured 4 mm with no intrahepatic biliary dilation, and the gallbladder showed cholelithiasis without wall thickening, collapsed around multiple calculi.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The report found neither CBD dilation (>6 mm) nor a CBD stone, nor gallbladder wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid, directly contradicting both branches of the expected finding.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stone) causing biliary obstruction and transient/ongoing hepatocellular injury
ensemble.information_gap: Whether a stone is lodged in the common bile duct (choledocholithiasis) causing biliary obstruction, which the ultrasound could not exclude — the CHD measured only 4 mm (not dilated) but the pancreas and distal CBD were not visualized due to overlying bowel gas
ensemble.expected_finding: A filling defect or stone within the common bile duct or intrahepatic ducts, or biliary ductal dilation not appreciated on ultrasound, confirming choledocholithiasis as the etiology of the markedly elevated transaminases and conjugated hyperbilirubinemia
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the non-invasive gold standard for visualizing the biliary tree and detecting choledocholithiasis when ultrasound is limited (pancreas obscured by bowel gas, distal CBD not seen) and LFTs are markedly elevated with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, directly informing the need for ERCP.
grounding: Bile Ducts: 'There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation. The CHD measures 4 mm.'
Pancreas: 'The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying bowel gas.'
Gallbladder: 'Cholelithiasis without definite gallbladder wall thickening. Gallbladder appears collapsed around multiple calculi.'
History: 'transferred to ___ for consideration of ERCP consultation given LFT abnormalities'
Lipase: '23.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — normal, making primary pancreatitis less likely
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has markedly elevated transaminases (ALT 528, AST 275), conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (direct bili 1.6), elevated alkaline phosphatase, and known cholelithiasis — a pattern highly consistent with choledocholithiasis causing biliary obstruction. The ultrasound showed a non-dilated CHD at 4 mm and failed to visualize the pancreas and distal CBD due to bowel gas, leaving the possibility of a passed or impacted CBD stone unresolved. MRCP is ordered to directly image the biliary tree and identify any obstructing stone, which would confirm the need for therapeutic ERCP and explain the LFT abnormalities.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stone) causing biliary obstruction / cholestatic hepatitis; less likely biliary stricture or ampullary pathology
ensemble.information_gap: Whether a stone (or other obstruction) is present in the common bile duct or distal biliary tree, which the ultrasound could not exclude because the CBD measured only 4 mm and the pancreas was not visualized due to bowel gas
ensemble.expected_finding: A filling defect or low-signal calculus within the common bile duct or common hepatic duct, or biliary dilation proximal to an obstructing lesion, confirming choledocholithiasis as the cause of the markedly elevated transaminases and conjugated hyperbilirubinemia
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The combination of known cholelithiasis, markedly elevated ALT/AST/ALP, conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, and a non-diagnostic ultrasound (pancreas obscured, CBD 4 mm but not definitively normal) creates a clear indication for MRCP to non-invasively evaluate the biliary tree before committing to ERCP.
grounding: Labs: ALT 528 IU/L (ref 0-40), AST 275 IU/L (ref 0-40), Alkaline Phosphatase 220 IU/L (ref 35-105), Bilirubin Total 2.3 mg/dL, Bilirubin Direct 1.6 mg/dL — cholestatic pattern with hepatocellular injury
Lipase: 23.0 IU/L (ref 0-60) — normal, making primary pancreatitis unlikely

Ultrasound: 'The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying bowel gas.'

History: 'transferred to ___ for consideration of ERCP consultation given LFT abnormalities'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has markedly elevated conjugated bilirubin and transaminases with known gallstones, a pattern strongly suggestive of choledocholithiasis causing biliary obstruction. The abdominal ultrasound confirmed gallstones but could not visualize the pancreas or adequately assess the distal common bile duct due to bowel gas, leaving the critical question of a CBD stone unanswered. MRCP is the appropriate next step to non-invasively image the biliary tree and either confirm a CBD stone (prompting ERCP) or identify an alternative biliary cause before proceeding to an invasive intervention.

ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a classic presentation of biliary colic with superimposed cholestatic liver injury (markedly elevated transaminases, direct hyperbilirubinemia, elevated alkaline phosphatase) in the setting of known cholelithiasis, raising high suspicion for choledocholithiasis. The abdominal ultrasound confirmed gallstones but could not adequately visualize the pancreas or distal CBD, and the CHD was only 4 mm — not overtly dilated but potentially underestimating obstruction in an early or partial stone impaction. MRCP is ordered to directly visualize the biliary tree and confirm or exclude a CBD stone, which would determine whether ERCP (already being considered) is indicated.

verification.actual_finding: MRCP demonstrated a focal filling defect in the mid to distal CBD (measuring up to 8 mm) consistent with choledocholithiasis, with no intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The MRCP directly confirmed the physician's primary expected finding of a CBD stone (choledocholithiasis) that was not visible on ultrasound, resolving the information gap and supporting pursuit of ERCP.
